directions.name: Empirical Validation of Fast-Slow Identity Learning (Theorem 1)
directions.motivation: Theorem 1 is the paper's most actionable formal claim, but depends on assumptions (A1, A2) that need empirical characterization. Companion papers (Deng et al. 2026a,b) address Performance/Efficiency but not Growth.
directions.key_question: Under what practical conditions does fast identity revision dominate slow-only learning?
directions.hypothesis: An agent with learned identity evolver ι will accumulate less regret than a fixed-identity agent across multiple rounds of slow updates + within-round identity adaptation.

minimum_viable_experiment.setting: Simplified grid-world or text-based task where identity encodes capability self-assessment (e.g., 'I can navigate mazes of complexity ≤ N')
independent_variables: Identity representation (scalar capability estimate / categorical role / vector embedding)
Identity evolver training (rule-based / learned from feedback)
dependent_variables: Cumulative regret (as defined in Eq. 9-10)
Per-step value difference Δ_t
Cross-round advantage η_k (Eq. 16)
Sensitivity to A1 violation (random vs adversarial identity updates)
baselines: Identity reset at each episode (no carry-over)
minimum_viable_experiment.expected_outcome: Fast-slow dominates in settings where identity revision is ≥55% accurate; gap grows with round count N_k as predicted.
minimum_viable_experiment.success_criteria: Statistically significant regret gap (p < 0.01) confirming Ω(∑N_k) bound; characterization of A1 failure regimes.

open_challenges: Defining identity space ℐ concretely
Training ι without ground-truth identity labels
Measuring 'closeness' of self-model d(i, i*)

directions.name: Effectiveness of World-Model-Based Augmentation (Theorem 2)
directions.motivation: Theorem 2 is central to the paper's critique of black-box policies. Testing it empirically reveals when and why world-model augmentation actually helps.
directions.key_question: Under what world-model quality (ε) and task characteristics does π_mix outperform the baseline policy π?
directions.hypothesis: Mixed policy π_mix (world-model-based planning fallback) improves any baseline policy when world model TV error ε ≤ ε_critical, with improvement proportional to task novelty/complexity.

minimum_viable_experiment.setting: Procedurally generated grid-world with controlled dynamics (familiar vs novel transitions)
independent_variables: World model accuracy ε (controlled via training data volume, 10% → 90% next-state accuracy)
Task novelty (in-distribution / mildly OOD / strongly OOD)
Planning horizon depth (1 step / 3 steps / 10 steps)
dependent_variables: V^g_{π_mix} - V^g_π (improvement over baseline)
Empirical gap from Theorem 2 bound
Planning invocation frequency (how often is π_mix ≠ π?)
Fraction of tasks where π_mix degrades performance
baselines: Baseline policy π (no world model, end-to-end RL)
Ensemble of policies (without structured mixed strategy)
minimum_viable_experiment.expected_outcome: π_mix matches or exceeds π when ε ≤ 0.2; improvement grows with task novelty; degradation risk near zero due to fallback mechanism.
minimum_viable_experiment.success_criteria: Confirm Theorem 2 prediction: V^g_{π_mix} ≥ V^g_π for all tested ε, with statistically significant advantage when ε ≤ 0.2.

open_challenges: Implementing the mixed policy selection mechanism (when to trust world model vs baseline)
Handling continuous action spaces
Measuring TV error between world model and true environment

directions.name: GIC Training Phase Ablation Study
directions.motivation: GIC's three-phase training (pretrain → simulative RL → deployment) is a central architectural claim but untested. An ablation study reveals which phases matter and how.
directions.key_question: What is the marginal contribution of each GIC training phase to final agentive capability?
directions.hypothesis: All three phases are necessary: phase 2 (simulative RL) provides the largest marginal gain, but phase 3 (deployment) is essential for sim-to-real robustness.

minimum_viable_experiment.setting: Minecraft-like or VirtualHome environment with physical and social components (e.g., 'cook a meal' requires navigation, tool use, coordination)
independent_variables: Training phase inclusion (phase 1 only / phases 1+2 / phases 1+2+3)
World model quality (accurate / moderately accurate / poor)
Task horizon (short: 10 steps / medium: 100 steps / long: 1000 steps)
dependent_variables: Task success rate
Sample efficiency (experience required for competence)
Generalization to unseen tasks/environments
Sim-to-real gap (performance drop from simulative test to deployment test)
baselines: End-to-end RL without phase distinction (monolithic training)
minimum_viable_experiment.expected_outcome: Full GIC (phases 1+2+3) significantly outperforms all ablations; phase 2 shows largest gain on long-horizon tasks; phase 3 critical for novel environments.
minimum_viable_experiment.success_criteria: Clear performance hierarchy: Full GIC > 1+2 > 1+3 > 2-only > end-to-end RL, with p < 0.01 on long-horizon tasks.

open_challenges: Building a sufficiently rich environment with both physical and social dimensions
Implementing all GIC components at even a prototype level
Controlling for model capacity across ablations
directions.related_sections: §5.3 Training the Agent Model, §5.2 GIC Architecture

directions.name: Configurator (System III) Training and Evaluation
directions.motivation: The configurator κ is the most novel component of GIC but the paper offers minimal guidance on how to train it. This is a high-precision open problem.
directions.key_question: How can κ learn to allocate deliberation optimally across diverse situations?
directions.hypothesis: A configurator trained via RL on a meta-reward (task success minus computation cost) will learn adaptive regulation policies that outperform fixed deliberation strategies.

minimum_viable_experiment.setting: Web navigation or text-based game with variable-difficulty tasks (easy: single step, hard: multi-hop reasoning)
independent_variables: Configurator training signal (meta-reward with computation cost weight: 0.0 / 0.1 / 0.5 / 1.0)
Regulation variable granularity (binary System I/II / fine-grained compute budget / hierarchical)
Task difficulty distribution (uniform / skewed easy / skewed hard)
dependent_variables: Accuracy vs computation Pareto frontier
Planning frequency vs task difficulty correlation
Overthinking rate (unnecessary deliberation on easy tasks)
Underthinking rate (insufficient deliberation on hard tasks)
baselines: Fixed threshold (plan if task takes > T expected steps)
GPT-5 style 'invisible model picker' (baseline from section 4.4)
minimum_viable_experiment.expected_outcome: RL-trained configurator achieves best Pareto frontier; planning frequency correlates with task difficulty; computation cost weight of 0.1-0.3 produces best trade-off.
minimum_viable_experiment.success_criteria: Learned configurator achieves ≥90% of optimal Pareto frontier with ≤20% overthinking rate; significantly outperforms all fixed baselines at computation-accuracy trade-off.

open_challenges: Defining computation cost function (FLOPs? latency? tokens?)
Avoiding configurator collapse (always choosing cheap mode)
Credit assignment across configurator decisions and downstream task outcomes

directions.name: PEG Evaluation Framework Application
directions.motivation: The PEG framework (Performance, Efficiency, Growth) is proposed to evaluate agentive systems. No concrete benchmark or protocol exists — this direction creates one.
directions.key_question: Can the PEG framework distinguish agentive systems from agentic ones on a standardized suite?
directions.hypothesis: PEG evaluation reveals systematic differences between agentive and agentic systems: both may score well on Performance, but only agentive systems score well on Growth.

minimum_viable_experiment.setting: Curated task suite across three tiers: procedural (fixed), adaptive (environment changes over time), and open-ended (no fixed termination)

Performance_test.goal_decomposition: Multi-stage tasks (e.g., 'prepare dinner for 4 with dietary restrictions')
Performance_test.identity_adaptation: Environment transfer tasks (change action space mid-deployment)
Performance_test.simulative_reasoning: Multi-constraint planning (e.g., 'find shortest path visiting A, B, C avoiding D')
Performance_test.reactive_execution: Real-time continuous control (e.g., object manipulation, driving)

Efficiency_test.latency_adaptation: Decision latency vs task difficulty correlation

Growth_test.learning_efficiency: Performance vs amount of real-world experience
Growth_test.self_directed_exploration: Performance when agent schedules own training vs fixed curriculum
Growth_test.learning_transfer: Performance gain on OOD tasks after in-distribution learning

agents_to_evaluate: Current agentic systems (Claude Code, Cursor, OpenClaw acting on tool-calling only)
minimum_viable_experiment.expected_outcome: PEG reveals clear separation: all systems score on Performance (varying levels); agentic systems show near-zero Growth; GIC prototypes show positive Growth curve.
minimum_viable_experiment.compute_budget: N/A (framework design + evaluation infrastructure: < 10 GPU-weeks to establish)
minimum_viable_experiment.success_criteria: PEG produces consistent, interpretable results across evaluation runs; Growth dimension differentiates agentive from agentic systems with d > 1.0 effect size.

open_challenges: Task suite coverage and bias
Standardizing environment interfaces across different agent architectures
Long evaluation cycles for Growth (requires many training iterations)

directions.name: Goal-Oriented Dataset Construction and Benchmarking
directions.motivation: The paper identifies goal-oriented data as 'the highest-leverage investment for training general-purpose agent models.' No such dataset currently exists at scale.
directions.key_question: What is the marginal value of goal annotations over raw trajectory data for training agent models?
directions.hypothesis: Goal-annotated trajectories provide substantially better training signal for hierarchical planning than raw trajectories alone, especially on long-horizon tasks.

minimum_viable_experiment.setting: Construct a dataset of 100K+ goal-annotated trajectories across multiple domains (household, office, games)

domains: Household: cooking, cleaning, organizing (from VirtualHome or BEHAVIOR)
Office: document processing, meeting scheduling (from task-oriented dialog datasets)
Games: Minecraft task completion, NetHack ascension runs

annotation_schema.subgoal_sequence: Ordered list of subgoals with timestamps

minimum_viable_experiment.evaluation: PEG Performance tasks requiring hierarchical planning
minimum_viable_experiment.expected_outcome: Goal-annotated training produces agents with 30%+ higher success on long-horizon tasks; subgoal hierarchy annotations provide most of the benefit.
minimum_viable_experiment.compute_budget: < 20 GPU-weeks for data generation + agent training
minimum_viable_experiment.success_criteria: Statistically significant improvement (p < 0.01) from goal annotations on long-horizon tasks; diminishing returns analysis showing 10% annotation is 80% as effective as 100%.

open_challenges: Scalable annotation pipeline (human annotation expensive; automated annotation needs validation)
Annotation consistency across domains
Ensuring goal variety covers broad objective space

directions.name: Safety Audit of GIC vs Monolithic Systems
directions.motivation: The paper's safety argument is entirely theoretical. An empirical comparison of failure modes between GIC-like modular architectures and monolithic systems would be highly impactful.
directions.key_question: Does GIC's layered transparency actually enable better failure detection and correction than monolithic approaches?
directions.hypothesis: Modular architectures (GIC) allow faster and more precise identification of failure sources, and more targeted correction, compared to monolithic policies.

minimum_viable_experiment.setting: Controlled sandbox environment with known safety violations (reward hacking, unsafe exploration, goal misgeneralization)
adversarial_scenarios: Reward hacking: agent discovers shortcut that maximizes reward without achieving goal
Goal misgeneralization: training goal differs from deployment goal in subtle ways
Instrumental subgoal emergence: self-preservation behavior conflicting with human termination
Distributional shift: deployment environment differs from training
independent_variables: Architecture type (monolithic black-box / modular GIC-like with explicit components)
Component auditability (all components inspectable / partial / none)
dependent_variables: Time to detect failure (interaction steps before anomaly flag)
Accuracy of root cause attribution (correct component identified)
Time to correct failure (interaction steps to repair and resume)
Side effects of correction (unintended behavior changes)
baselines: Modular with no auditability (components exist but opaque)
minimum_viable_experiment.expected_outcome: GIC's modular architecture enables 2-5× faster failure detection and 3-10× faster correction compared to monolithic baselines.
minimum_viable_experiment.success_criteria: Statistically significant advantage (p < 0.01) in detection time, attribution accuracy, and correction time for GIC-style architecture across all four scenarios.

open_challenges: Building a representative GIC prototype for safety testing
Designing adversarial scenarios that are challenging but diagnostic
Controlling for confounds (modular systems may detect failures via simpler heuristics)

directions.name: World Model vs Agent Model Separation: Empirical Impact
directions.motivation: A core principle of the paper is functional separation of world model and agent model. Many recent proposals (DreamZero, Cosmos) collapse them. This is a falsifiable architectural claim.
directions.key_question: Does keeping the world model functionally separate from the agent model measurably improve performance, safety, or training efficiency?
directions.hypothesis: Separate training (WM on prediction error, AM on reward) produces more reliable planning and more interpretable failures than joint world-action models.

minimum_viable_experiment.setting: Continuous control task (MuJoCo or DM Control) with known transition dynamics where planning accuracy is critical
independent_variables: Architecture (separate WM + AM / joint world-action model / collapsed model)
Training signal separation (separate losses / shared loss / shared weights)
Task type (deterministic dynamics / stochastic dynamics / partially observable)
dependent_variables: Planning accuracy (predicted next-state error under chosen actions)
Task success rate
Interpretability score (human rater: can failure be attributed to agent or world model?)
Training efficiency (experience required to reach threshold performance)
baselines: GIC-style separate WM+AM with information exchange only through activations
minimum_viable_experiment.expected_outcome: Separate models achieve comparable or better task performance, with significantly better interpretability and cleaner training gradients.
minimum_viable_experiment.success_criteria: Separate-model architecture demonstrates statistically significant advantage in interpretability (Cohen's d > 0.8) without significant performance degradation (≤ 5% from best collapsed model).

open_challenges: Implementing a fair comparison where model capacity is controlled across architectures
Defining and measuring 'interpretability' objectively
Handling the coupling through activation exchange (which may introduce its own issues)
